speaker: interviewer
text: You're doing a very good job, Anna.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, really?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's very natural.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. And you're doing-- both-- all of you today a good job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anna, tell me about what life was like for you when you arrived in America.

speaker: interviewee
text: The reason that I came here, take care of the very sick person, Joe Perl, my brother-in-law. He-- he did have cancer on his prostate. And he was not married. He was a single man, living here in this house. And he had some friends who helped him. But he needed, really, help, because he was very, very sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: So firmly decided that I am the person who have to go. Because my husband, they wouldn't go along, first of all. Secondly, he not know whom can take care of sick person. So they decided I go. And it was for me very-- very hard, to leave my children. And you know who I have left, only the children, and to leave them and my husband. They came here, but I-- I didn't have another choice. I have to go. So your family followed you within the year.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they arrived.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was less than a year, less than a year. And they arrived here, and the boys-- it was summer. It was a vacation, so they went to-- to school to First School-- [INAUDIBLE] high school. And Julius, he was always a very good student. And the younger one-- Paul also was very good. But he jump one year. And even they didn't know English. And he-- he could make it in this high school. He jump one year. So together with Paul, they-- they went to the high school, First Colonial High School. And when was parents ask how they doing, they said they have very good mind. They doing very good, especially in mathematic. And then they study-- they graduate in magna cum laude, both of them. And I was so proud of them. And then one is get to MIT, Julius, the youngest. And Paul went to University of Virginia. He met his-- his wife there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do they do today for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: They are in computers. Julius has his own company with two other guys. And-- and Paul work for cable wireless. He's the senior engineer in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you arrived in this country, did you speak about your wartime experiences with anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't I even didn't-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: I even didn't-- I tell them that Josh is not here. It's OK. It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- I even-- you ask me whether I talk about my experience. I even didn't talk to my family really, to nobody. I-- I didn't want make them sad, and I didn't like to talk about war. I did not read the books, not see the movies, absolutely not. But then come time, especially when the movie come out that everybody was talking about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you-- when you were not speaking about it to anyone, did you think about it to yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course I been thinking about my family that didn't survive. Questions I have, why I survive, why they didn't survive. And-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you discussed it with no one?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you start discussing it with people and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just if they are ask me to-- to have the speeches.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they ask you for the speeches?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they heard I-- I was in Schindler camp. I'm Schindler survivor. First camp, [INAUDIBLE] to me, you know the-- from Channel 13? She was the first one. And she interview me. And then interview me and from-- from [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this after the film

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did things change for you, once that film was released?

speaker: interviewee
text: It changed that people want talk, that I have speeches and-- this make me busy. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Doing what kinds of things?

speaker: interviewee
text: The speeches.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whom do you speak to?

speaker: interviewee
text: In school, in schools, middle school, like 11 graders, 12 graders.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you say to the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: First-- first I didn't know how to talk. So-- so just, I talk like I talk to you. And they say that they learn more from my speech than they learn in-- they read books. It's easier for them, history, you know? And you saw the thank you letters what they send to me. I have real letters, read a lot from before.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you start speaking to your children about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- when I start to-- to talk. It was after the movie come out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the movie their first idea--

speaker: interviewee
text: But they don't ask me questions, how it was. They know that I don't like to talk about it. They-- they don't ask any questions, just more they find out from other people than from me.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you go to the schools and you speak to the children, what do you tell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just I tell how-- how-- what I went through. First, where I was born. I would talk about how nice was life, the skiing and the tourism, and climbing the mountains. And then I-- I told them what happened and-- and they're so quiet when I speak. I-- I speak quiet voice like now. And it's so quiet. They're so concentrated and listen. And then I-- they-- they have questions, what questions? So I-- I have the satisfaction that I-- I give something to the people some.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's an example of a good question they ask you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] They ask a question how-- how I survive and who survived from my family and also about my beliefs, where I believe in God, stuff like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you believe about God today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe in God. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I pray to God. Are you in communication with your cousin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was here in November, visiting. I was there, too, visiting him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other members of your family who survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's much older than-- than I am. He's in the 80s. Other year-- one cousin, a woman, she is in Florida now. And my aunt, her mother, she passed away. And the cousin, I-- her and her brother, probably not too much in touch. Not so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do do you keep busy today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just take care of the house, and I have a garden and take care of Josh, many times [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is Josh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just is my grandson, babysit him. And I go visit Paul sometime. I [INAUDIBLE] Passover I spent with Julius. So visiting the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anna, is there a message you would like to leave with us today, for the children and for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my message would be that prejudice is very wrong. You're not supposed to be prejudice. And that just laugh to each other. This is important that hate is [INAUDIBLE] stuff. And like the-- the skinheads and the organization, this fascists and the Neo-Nazi, not supposed to exist. And what I think, I-- I understand it's democracy and everybody can do whatever he believe in. But I think after the all what we went through, not supposed to be organization like Neo-Nazi or skinheads or-- or-- and fight prejudicism [INAUDIBLE]. Just, there-- they have never [INAUDIBLE] love and love people and help people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your general feeling about the state of affairs in the world today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, state affairs?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you see things today that remind you, other than the Neo-Nazis, about things that happened before and during the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, like Yugoslavia. Yeah, I see is very wrong, very wrong. Especially Yugoslavia, because they fight brother against brother. It's bad enough that they-- they fight the races, the races [INAUDIBLE]. But-- but brother against brother is a [INAUDIBLE]. I-- I think people have to do something about that, to help them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anna, I want to thank you very much for sharing this important story with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you're welcome. Oh, you're very welcome. And thank you very much for coming and to you and to you, to helping Linda and making the-- the tape. I will get one, I hope.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Sophia Duklauer and my father Julius Duklauer And-- and-- what-- what? Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father and a person, this other man, he worked for my father. My brother and myself, and we climb very high mountains here. And down was a big leg and was very scary to look down. And but was a beautiful nature and this what we-- we like to do in summer. And in winter, we ski. This is my mother, my father, myself to the right with the-- this is my sister and-- and my brother, Morris.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my cousin, Herman, he live in Canada, Montreal. This was his sister, Anna, his little brother, Morris, and [? Etela, ?] his youngest sister. They-- they-- he's in Canada. He's alive. He survived, but [INAUDIBLE], they didn't survive the war. This is my brother, Morris. He was here maybe 10 years old. This is my husband, John, myself, and my three children, Paul, this one, Sonia, and-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is my cousin Herman from Canada, from Montreal, and myself right away after the war when we met. I still have no shoes. These are very old shoes. But I always have the only one. This is my grandmother, Rosa. I don't know how old is she here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rosa Goldberger. Better

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, the ski trip.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is the skiing.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]. There must be something weird with that switch. We're still recording. That's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was blonde. This is my brother, Morris, my birth girlfriend, Miriam, my [INAUDIBLE] and my sister Erna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yep. [INAUDIBLE] You're recording.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is myself, Steve Spielberg, and Mr. [? Lerner. ?] He's the director of the Holocaust. We light the candles here for the six million perished Jewish people. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is myself, Steve Spielberg, Mr. [? Lerner, ?] and Mrs. Emilie Schindler [INAUDIBLE] of Oskar Schindler. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This is miss-- First Lady Mrs. Clinton. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mrs. [? Schutz, ?] [? Lynn ?] [? Schutz ?] is [? Laurie's ?] mother, [? Laurie ?] and myself. And we been seeing the first time the movie, the premiere, of And then we been invited to White House. And this is the oval room. It's the oval room. This is myself, First Lady Hillary Clinton, [? Laurie, ?] my daughter-in-law, Paul's wife, and her mother, then Mrs. [? Lynn ?] [? Schutz. ?] And we are in the White House.

speaker: interviewer
text: Smile, Sonia. Don't forget your [INAUDIBLE]. [LAUGHTER] Shh. Jonathan, look at the camera. [INAUDIBLE], please. Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now? My name is Anna Perl, and this is my family-- Tali, Sonia, Yael, Danny, Paul, and [? Laurie. ?] Then my grandchildren-- Michele, Deborah, Jonathan, and Joshua. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] I have another son, my youngest son Julius Perl, he live in Massachusetts, in Burlington. And I wish that all my family will live in peace so they don't experience times like I did, just they will live-- live in peace and in happiness. This is my wish. [PAUSES FOR 22 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Same thing? I wish that my youngest son, Julius Perl, is here with us, with his family. His wife, [? Charlene, ?] and the three kids, Olivia, Joseph, and Arielle. It's OK, guys. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Hey. Say something.

speaker: interviewer
text: She heard. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi, everyone.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you, Josh, make-- make-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Say hi, Josh. Wave-- wave to the camera.

speaker: interviewer
text: Get up and wave.

speaker: interviewee
text: He doesn't have to?

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] the camera.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She did [INAUDIBLE]. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Say bye.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bye!

speaker: interviewee
text: Bye!
